comment:
Use the audio function as a memory inspector. The Addr knob (0..15) selects a 4-byte offset; the audio function reads *(0xc009c1a0 + 4*N) directly and turns the low 8 bits of the read value into a 0..2 gain. As the user turns the knob, different firmware-RAM globals are sampled. The settings where gain changes when tap tempo is pressed point at BPM-related storage.

state[31]'s table base 0xc009c1a0 is the per-slot scratch we already mapped (B4=2 returns knob 1 value, etc.). Reading PAST the per-slot rows reaches adjacent firmware globals. With knob = 0..15 we sweep 0xc009c1a0..0xc009c1dc — the same range that nearby firmware code references (c00a224c references 0xc009c1d4 etc).

Test plan:
  1. Load probe, unbypass — expect SOMETHING audible (default knob = 0 reads the state[31] table base pointer; should give a non-zero gain).
  2. Sweep the knob through 0..15, listening at each setting. Some settings will read pointers (huge values, gain saturates), some will be near-zero, some may be small ints.
  3. At each setting, tap the tempo button at a steady BPM, then a different BPM. If the audio gain CHANGES with tap tempo at a particular knob setting, that knob value points at BPM-related memory.
  4. The interesting knob N -> address 0xc009c1a0 + 4*N. Record it.

params.name: Addr
params.comment: Knob 0..100 -> address index 0..15 (clamped). Maps via low-bit truncation.
